106	# Blueprint Reviewer
107	
108	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
109	
110	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
111	
112	## Your Job
113	
114	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
115	
116	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
117	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
118	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
119	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
120	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
121	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
122	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
123	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
124	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
125	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
126	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
127	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
128	
129	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
130	
131	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
132	
133	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
134	
135	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
136	
137	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematicaly correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
138	
139	## Always read everything
140	
141	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
142	
143	## Directive Format
144	
145	```markdown
146	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
147	
148	## Slug
149	<slug>
150	
151	## Strategy snapshot
152	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy.>
153	
154	## Routes
155	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
156	
157	## References
158	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
159	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
160	
161	## Focus areas (optional)
162	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
163	
164	## Known issues
165	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
166	```
167	
168	## What you do
169	
170	1. **Read your directive completely.**
171	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
172	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
173	   - Read the entire chapter.
174	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
175	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
176	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
177	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
178	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
179	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
180	7. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
181	
182	You may also use:
183	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
184	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
185	
186	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
187	
188	## Report format
189	
190	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
191	
192	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
193	
194	Concretely:
195	
196	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
197	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
198	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
199	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
200	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
201	
202	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
203	
204	```markdown
205	# Blueprint Review Report
206	
207	## Slug
208	<slug>
209	
210	## Iteration
211	<NNN>
212	
213	## Top-level summaries
214	
215	### Incomplete parts
216	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
217	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
218	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
219	
220	### Proofs lacking detail
221	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
222	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
223	
224	### Lean difficulty quality
225	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
226	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
227	
228	### Multi-route coverage
229	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
230	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
231	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
232	
233	### Citation discipline
234	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
235	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
236	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
237	- `Baz.tex` / `\def:etale`: `% SOURCE QUOTE:` is in English but the cited source is [EGA IV, §17.6] (French). Quote was translated — re-extract verbatim in French.
238	- `Qux.tex` / `\thm:gaga`: `% SOURCE QUOTE:` uses the project's notation $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ throughout but the cited source [Serre, GAGA] uses $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. Quote was rewritten — re-extract verbatim with original notation.
239	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before `\begin{proof}` — proof prose reads as a direct translation of Hartshorne's argument; should have the verbatim source proof in a comment.
240	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: visible `\textit{Source: ...}` line claims `Hartshorne III.6.2` but `% SOURCE:` pointer says `III.5.1`. Pointer drift — one of them is wrong.
241	- `Foo.tex`: `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/vakil-ch24.md)` in 3 blocks, but the writer's report's "References consulted" list does not mention `vakil-ch24.md`. The writer cited a file they did not open — fabrication.
242	
243	## Per-chapter
244	
245	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
246	- **complete**: true | partial | false
247	- **correct**: true | partial | false
248	- **notes**:
249	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
250	  - ...
251	
252	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex
253	- **complete**: ...
254	- **correct**: ...
255	- **notes**:
256	  - ...
257	
258	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
259	block above. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
260	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner from the omit-empty
261	rule — do NOT pad them out with `notes: -` or `notes: no drift`.)
262	
263	## Cross-chapter notes
264	
265	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
266	- `<chapter A>` `\lean{Foo.bar}` references a declaration that no longer exists in `Foo.lean` (renamed?).
267	- ...
268	
269	(Use this section for findings that span multiple chapters. Omit if empty.)
270	
271	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
272	
273	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here. These take precedence over everything else; the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md before any Lean work this iter.
274	
275	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>. Resolving requires either changing the strategy or redefining `foo`.
276	
277	## Severity summary
278	
279	Apply these rules verbatim — they decide whether the plan agent dispatches a blueprint-writing subagent (per the catalog) this iter, or defers.
280	
281	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
282	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
283	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
284	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter. A `partial` chapter cannot be relied on by any prover; the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent must be dispatched.
285	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
286	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels — these silently corrupt the dependency graph and must be fixed before provers downstream of them run.
287	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**: a missing or suspicious-looking `% SOURCE QUOTE:` on a definition / theorem whose `\lean{...}` is in PROGRESS.md. Formalizing an unverified statement is the iter-149 failure mode; the catalog's literature/reference-fetching subagent must be dispatched before the prover runs.
288	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
289	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
290	  - Citation-discipline findings on blocks NOT feeding an active prover route — still must be resolved eventually, but the project can ship one more iter without them.
291	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues, missing `\texttt{...}` decoration mentions, etc.
292	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
293	
294	Overall verdict: one sentence.
295	```

297	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter (see dispatcher_notes for the gate rule). Do not under-classify to avoid blocking provers — the project pays more when a prover formalizes against a wrong/incomplete blueprint than when the prover waits one iter for a writer to land the fix.

For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
50	
51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54	
55	\leanok
56	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
57	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
59	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
60	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
61	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
62	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
63	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
64	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
65	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
68	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
72	\end{lemma}
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
75	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
76	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
77	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
78	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
79	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
80	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
81	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
82	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
83	% assertion."

111	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
112	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
113	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
114	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
115	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
116	
117	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
118	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
119	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
120	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
121	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
122	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
123	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
124	
125	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
126	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
127	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
128	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
129	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
130	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
131	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
132	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
133	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
134	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
135	\end{proof}

140	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
141	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
142	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
143	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
144	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
145	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
146	  irreducible.)

319	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $X$, $Y$, $Z$ be objects of
320	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ with $X$ proper, $X \times Y$ geometrically irreducible and
321	  reduced, and $Z$ separated. Let $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a $\bar k$-point
322	  $x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X$, and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,

326	    \item $U \subseteq X \times Y$ is a $p_2$-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$
327	      for a set $V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y$ (so $U$ contains the whole fibre $X_y$ over each of
328	      its points);

336	\begin{proof}
337	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each $y \in V$, the complete
338	  variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single
339	  point of $U$''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
340	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
341	  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
342	  $H^0$ base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument has two steps.
343	
344	  \medskip
345	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy).} Fix a closed point $y \in V_{\mathrm{set}}$.
346	  Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and $Y$ is of finite type over $\bar k$, the residue field
347	  is $\kappa(y) = \bar k$. Because $U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})$ is saturated, $U$ contains the
348	  \emph{entire} fibre $X_y \cong X$ over $y$; hence $f$ maps the proper integral slice $X_y$ into the
349	  affine $U_0$. By $\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$ the global sections $\Gamma(X_y)$ form a
350	  field, module-finite over $\bar k$ by $\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$, and
351	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into
352	  the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring map on global sections ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), so the
353	  restriction of $f$ to $X_y$ factors through a single $\bar k$-point of $U_0$ --- necessarily the
354	  point $f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$. Thus $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$
355	  agree at every closed point of $U$.
356	
357	  \medskip
358	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points).} The closed points are dense in
359	  the locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-scheme $U$ ($\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}$ / the Jacobson-space
360	  property). To turn ``$f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree at each closed point'' into a
361	  single morphism equality on $U$, we assemble the closed points into one dominant probe --- the
362	  coproduct $\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U$, whose range is dense ---
363	  and feed it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle
364	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'}$ (the same handle $\mathtt{rigidity\_core}$ uses).
365	  This yields the morphism equality $U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)$
366	  on all of $U$. The ``agrees on a dense set of closed points $\Rightarrow$ hom-equality'' connective
367	  is the one piece Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
368	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$); building the dense-range witness over the closed points is a small
369	  bespoke step, \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
370	\end{proof}

433	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.

435	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
436	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
437	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
438	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
439	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).

441	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
442	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
443	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
444	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
445	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
446	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
447	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only

454	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)

491	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
492	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
493	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
494	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
495	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in

498	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
499	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
500	\end{proposition}
501	
502	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
503	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
504	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
505	\begin{proof}
506	  \uses{def:genus}
507	  \leanok
508	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
509	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
510	
511	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
512	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
513	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
514	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
515	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
516	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
517	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
518	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
519	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
520	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
521	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
522	\end{proof}

548	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
549	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
550	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
551	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
552	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
553	
554	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
555	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
556	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
557	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
558	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
559	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
560	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
561	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
562	  \eta[A]$.

565	\begin{proof}
566	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
567	  \leanok
568	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
569	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
570	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
571	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
572	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
573	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
574	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
575	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
576	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
577	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$. It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X$ be a reduced geometrically irreducible $k$-scheme and let $Y$ be a separated $k$-scheme. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms of $k$-schemes, and let $U \subseteq X$ be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where $U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X$ is the canonical open immersion), then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms of $k$-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where $Y$ is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ formed from the group law on $Y$. The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side $[\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ to $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$.
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{$Y \to \Spec k$ is separated.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ provides this directly (no projection from $\mathtt{IsProper}$ needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form $\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)$ that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{$X$ is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]$ over the field-spectrum base $\Spec k$ (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields $\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}$ via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion $U.\iota$ is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]$, the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality $g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}$ from the agreement-on-$U$ hypothesis. The promotion to $g_1 = g_2$ in $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$ uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of $X.\mathrm{left}$.
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion $U.\iota$ from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to $\mathrm{Over}$-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on $X$ and $Y$ are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-$\bar k$ argument (for $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ hitting the identity at a $\bar k$-point) to this theorem with $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $Y := A_{\bar k}$. The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of $\Jac(C)$): given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, scheme-level agreement on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of \ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	
4	Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $k$ in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus $g = g(C)$.
5	
6	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of $C$ in the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine $g$-dimensional abelian variety even when $C(k) = \emptyset$ (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus $0$ the witness object is instead the trivial scheme $\Spec k$, \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the rigidity-lemma $+$ theorem-of-the-cube chain, \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-$\bar k$ declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries $[\mathtt{CharZero}]$, is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-$0$ witness consumes.
7	
8	\section{The Albanese construction}
9	
10	For a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$, the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from $C$. Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
11	
12	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
13	
14	Let $C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)$ be the $g$\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of $C$ (the quotient of $C^g$ by the action of the symmetric group $S_g$). It is a smooth proper scheme over $k$ of dimension $g$. Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
15	\[
16	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
17	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
18	\]
19	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing $P_0$ gives a translation isomorphism $\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}$, and the Stein factorisation of $\sigma$ yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
20	
21	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of $C$ inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
22	
23	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
24	
25	\begin{definition}\leanok
26	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
27	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
28	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
29	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
30	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
31	  % such $b$ arises from a unique map $a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}$ such that
32	  % $a(0)=0$.  Since $B^*$ is integral, $a$ factors through $P$.  Thus the
33	  % maps $a\:B^*\to P$ and $b\:X\to B$ are in bijective correspondence.
34	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps $b'\:B\to B'$ such
35	  % that $b'(0)=0$.  In particular, $1_P$ corresponds to a natural map
36	  % $u\:X\to A$ such that $u(x)=0$, and every map $b\:X\to B$ factors
37	  % uniquely through $u$."
38	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
39	  Let $(C, P)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with a marked $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$. A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ is an \emph{Albanese object} for $(C, P)$ if there exists a morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ sending $P$ to the identity element $\eta \in J$, and such that every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ factors uniquely as $f = \iota \circ g$ for some morphism of group schemes $g \colon J \to A$.
40	\end{definition}
41	
42	\begin{remark}
43	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
44	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
45	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on $J$ --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on $\iota$ and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on $J$ sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on $\Jac(C)$ before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety $A$ in the universal-property clause.
46	\end{remark}
47	
48	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
49	
50	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term $h$ packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition $P \circ \iota = \eta_J$ and the universal-factorisation property of $\iota$. The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
51	
52	\begin{definition}
53	
54	\leanok
55	[The Albanese universal morphism]
56	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
58	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
59	  Given a pointed curve $(C, P)$ and an Albanese term $h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$, the \emph{universal morphism} of $h$ is the morphism
60	  \[
61	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
62	  \]
63	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of $h$.
64	\end{definition}
65	
66	\begin{lemma}
67	
68	\leanok
69	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
70	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
72	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
73	  The universal morphism $h.\mathtt{ofCurve}$ sends the marked point $P$ to the identity of $J$:
74	  \[
75	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
76	  \]
77	\end{lemma}
78	
79	\begin{lemma}
80	
81	\leanok
82	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
83	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
84	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
85	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
86	  For every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, there exists a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g$.
87	\end{lemma}
88	
89	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point $P \in C(k)$ as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that $P$, and applies the corresponding helper above.
90	
91	\begin{theorem}\leanok
92	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
93	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
95	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
96	  Let $J_1, J_2$ be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve $(C, P)$, with universal pointed morphisms $\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1$ and $\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2$ (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism $e \colon J_1 \to J_2$ satisfying $\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e$. The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation $\mathrm{id}$, and the produced morphism $e$ is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of $e$ as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
97	\end{theorem}
98	
99	\begin{remark}
100	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
101	  \uses{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
102	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism $h \colon J_2 \to J_1$ and verifies $g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}$ and $h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}$ (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple $(g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})$. The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
103	  \[
104	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
105	  \]
106	  The natural strengthening would be to return
107	  \[
108	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
109	  \]
110	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
111	\end{remark}
112	
113	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
114	
115	\begin{definition}\leanok
116	[Jacobian]
117	  \label{def:Jacobian}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
119	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
120	  The \emph{Jacobian} of $C$ is the abelian variety $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ obtained as the Albanese object of $C$: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ of dimension $g(C)$ equipped with a universal morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$. Formally, $\Jac(C)$ is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-$P$) Albanese witness for $C$ provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
121	
122	  When $g(C) = 0$ the Jacobian collapses to $\Spec k$ itself, which is the terminal object in the category of $k$-schemes. When $g(C) > 0$ the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers $C$ itself (after choosing a $k$-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: $\Jac(C)$ is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-$0$ specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over $k$ of relative dimension $0$ is isomorphic to $\Spec k$.
123	\end{definition}
124	
125	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
126	
127	The four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
128	
129	\begin{theorem}\leanok
130	[Group object structure]
131	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
133	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
134	  $\Jac(C)$ carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	\begin{proof}
138	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, thm:IsAlbanese_unique}

140	
141	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if $\iota \colon C \to J$ is the universal morphism sending the marked point $P$ to the identity, then the diagonal $C \times C \to J \times J$, post-composed with the universal morphism out of $C \times C$ (factored through $J$ by Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. 142	\end{proof}

146	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
148	  \uses{def:Jacobian, def:genus}
149	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Cor.~cor:sm (smoothness/dimension over a field) + Cor.~cor:ch0 (char-$0$ smoothness) + Prp.~prp:P0 (smooth over reduced $S$); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~34, 38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)

162	  % has an open group subscheme $\IPicz_{X/S}$ of finite type whose fibers
163	  % are the $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$.  Furthermore, if $S$ is reduced, then
164	  % $\IPicz_{X/S}$ is smooth over $S$."
165	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor.~(cor:sm), Cor.~(cor:ch0), Prp.~(prp:P0).}
166	  $\Jac(C) \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$.
167	\end{theorem}
168	
169	\begin{proof}
170	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, def:genus}
171	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$; the relative dimension $g(C)$ is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
172	
173	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of $C$ equals the genus $g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to $\Jac(C)$ at the identity is canonically identified with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of $\mathcal O_C$ is classified by an element of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$, and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$. Smoothness of $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any $k$-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of $\Jac(C)$ at every $k$-rational point is the same as at the identity.
174	\end{proof}

176	\begin{theorem}\leanok
177	[Properness]
178	  \label{thm:Jacobian_proper}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper}
180	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
181	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Prp.~prp:P0 ($\IPicz_{X/S}$ closed in $\IPic_{X/S}$ and proper over $S$ when fibres are complete); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
182	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, and represents

185	  % subscheme $\IPicz_{X/S}$ of finite type whose fibers are the

187	  % is smooth over $S$.  Moreover, if all the $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ are complete
188	  % and if $\IPic_{X/S}$ is separated over $S$, then $\IPicz_{X/S}$ is closed
189	  % in $\IPic_{X/S}$ and proper over $S$."
190	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Prp.~(prp:P0).}
191	  $\Jac(C) \to \Spec k$ is proper.
192	\end{theorem}
193	
194	\begin{proof}
195	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
196	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{proper} : \mathrm{IsProper}\, J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness.
197	
198	  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, which is itself proper over $\Spec k$ because $C$ is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for $\Pic$ of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ (and hence $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ after translation) follows from properness of $\Sym^g(C)$ together with the universal closedness of the surjection $\sigma$. In either case, properness descends to $\Jac(C)$.
199	\end{proof}

202	[Geometric irreducibility]
203	  \label{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible}
205	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
206	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Lem.~lem:agps (the identity component $G^0$ is open-closed, finite type, geometrically irreducible, commutes with field extension) + Prp.~prp:pic0 (specialisation to $\IPicz_{X/k}$); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~30, 31  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)

208	  % locally of finite type.  Let $G^0$ denote the connected component of the
209	  % identity element $e$.  \tu{(1)} Then $G$ is separated.  \tu{(2)} Then $G$
210	  % is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open subscheme.  \tu{(3)}
211	  % Then $G^0$ is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it is
212	  % geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending $k$."

218	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
219	  % extending $k$."

222	\end{theorem}

224	\begin{proof}
225	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
226	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{geomIrred} : \mathrm{GeometricallyIrreducible}\, J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness.
227	
228	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety $\Jac(C)$ is the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$ (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, $\Jac(C)$ arises as the Stein factorisation of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ followed by a translation; $\Sym^g(C)$ is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of $C^g$, itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.
229	\end{proof}
230	
231	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
232	
233	\begin{itemize}
234	  \item $g = 0$: Then $\Jac(C) = \Spec k$, the trivial group scheme; $\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $0 = g$.
235	  \item $g = 1$: Then $\Jac(C) \cong C$ once a $k$-rational point on $C$ is fixed; $C$ is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension $1$).
236	  \item $g \ge 2$: $\Jac(C)$ is a non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to $C$ as a scheme.
237	\end{itemize}
238	
239	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
240	
241	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.
242	
243	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
244	
245	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of $C$. This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
246	
247	\begin{definition}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[Albanese witness for a curve]
251	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
253	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
254	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ is a bundle of the following seven fields:
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item $J$: an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$ --- the candidate Albanese object.
257	    \item $\mathtt{grpObj}$: a group-object structure on $J$ in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
258	    \item $\mathtt{proper}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is proper.
259	    \item $\mathtt{smooth}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth.
260	    \item $\mathtt{geomIrred}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
261	    \item $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$. This refines $\mathtt{smooth}$ at the specific relative dimension $\genus\, C$ (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
262	    \item $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$: a proof that, for every $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
263	  \end{itemize}
264	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
265	\end{definition}
266	
267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
269	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
270	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
271	  \[
272	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
273	  \]
274	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single $J$ is chosen once (intrinsic to $C$), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
275	  \[
276	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
277	  \]
278	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to $C$: a different choice of $P \in C(k)$ alters the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism of $J$, and so leaves $J$ unchanged as a scheme. 279	\end{remark}
